file_name: Maniraj and Sardarmaran - 2021 - Classification of dermoscopic images using soft computing techniques.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Deep Learning Neural Networks (DLNN) for the classification of dermoscopic images to diagnose skin cancer (melanoma). The methodology includes image preprocessing, feature extraction using 3DWT, feature selection using Genetic Algorithms (GA), and classification using a DLNN classifier on the PH2 and ISIC 2017 databases.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and presents a new Computer-Aided Diagnosis (CAD) system. It details original experimental methods including image preprocessing, feature extraction using 3DWT, feature selection via Genetic Algorithms, and classification using a Deep Learning Neural Network (DLNN). The paper reports original quantitative results, such as 98.67% accuracy on the PH2 database and 94.50% on the ISIC 2017 database, which confirms it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: Classification of dermoscopic images using soft computing techniques
brief_summary: This study presents a Computer-Aided Diagnosis system for classifying dermoscopic images to diagnose skin cancer, specifically melanoma. The methodology employs soft computing techniques, including median filtering for preprocessing, 3-dimensional Discrete Wavelet Transform (3DWT) for feature extraction, and Genetic Algorithms (GA) for feature selection. The selected features are classified using a Deep Learning Neural Network (DLNN) classifier. The system was evaluated on the PH2 and ISIC 2017 databases, achieving a maximum accuracy of 98.67% on the PH2 dataset and 94.50% on the ISIC 2017 dataset. These results demonstrate that the proposed soft computing approach outperforms several existing methods in skin cancer classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Classified as a malignant class in the PH2 dataset (40 images total: 20 training, 20 testing) and the ISIC 2017 dataset. The study focuses on diagnosing skin cancer, specifically melanoma.
Classified as a benign class in the PH2 dataset (80 images total: 40 training, 40 testing).
Classified as a benign class in the PH2 dataset (80 images total: 40 training, 40 testing).

all_task_types.target: Dermoscopic image categories (Common Nevus, Atypical Nevus, Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs a 3-class classification of dermoscopic images into benign (Common Nevus, Atypical Nevus) and malignant (Melanoma) classes using a Deep Learning Neural Network (DLNN) classifier. The task is explicitly defined as 'skin cancer classification' and 'dermoscopic image categorization' with reported accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Database
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image database used for skin cancer diagnosis. The study utilized images from this database for training and testing the proposed soft computing classification system.

use.training: Used for training the Deep Learning Neural Network (DLNN) classifier with selected features from Genetic Algorithms.
use.testing: Used for testing the DLNN classifier to evaluate performance metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017 Database
datasets_used.description: The International Skin Imaging Collaboration 2017 database, a benchmark dataset containing dermoscopic images. The study used a subset of this database for external validation of the proposed system.

use.testing: Used for testing the proposed system to compare performance against other existing CNN-based approaches.
use.other: The full training set of 2000 images was mentioned as part of the database description, but the study specifically evaluated performance on 750 testing images using features extracted via 3rd-level DWT.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compute True Positives (TP), True Negatives (TN), False Positives (FP), and False Negatives (FN) for calculating accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.
To compare the performance of the proposed system against existing methods (e.g., KNN, SVM, NN ensemble) using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics.
To investigate the effect of control parameters 'a' and 'b' in the Genetic Algorithm fitness function on system performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity metrics derived from confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

PH2 Database.notes: The proposed system achieved the highest performance on the PH2 database. The 3rd-level 3DWT representation yielded the best results (98.67% accuracy). The GA parameters were optimized with a=0.8 and b=0.2.

ISIC 2017 Database.notes: The proposed system achieved the highest average accuracy (94.50%) on the ISIC 2017 testing set (750 images) compared to existing CNN-based approaches. Features were extracted using 3rd-level DWT.

architecture_used.input_size: Input features selected by Genetic Algorithm (GA) from 3DWT; original image size 768 x 560 pixels

architecture_details.model_type: Deep Neural Network (subdivision of Machine Learning)
architecture_details.description: A deep learning approach with more hidden layers than traditional networks to model nonlinear relationships. The number of hidden layers was increased to ten times that of a simple network with backpropagation descent algorithm.

training_details.training_data_source: PH2 database (100 images: 40 Common Nevus, 40 Atypical Nevus, 20 Melanoma)
training_details.feature_selection: Genetic Algorithm (GA) with population size 500, mutation rate 0.02, crossover rate 0.7, elitism strategy, and termination at 500 generations or no change in 50 generations

preprocessing_techniques: Median filtering for noise and hair removal (artifact removal)
Window size optimization for median filter (21 x 21 pixels used to effectively remove hairs while preserving image details)
conclusions: The proposed Computer-Aided Diagnosis system, utilizing 3DWT for feature extraction, Genetic Algorithms for feature selection, and a Deep Learning Neural Network (DLNN) for classification, achieved a maximum accuracy of 98.67% on the PH2 database.
The system achieved an average accuracy of 94.50% on the ISIC 2017 database, outperforming existing CNN-based approaches such as Wavelet + ResNet, Gabor + ResNet, and Hybrid CNNs.
The 3rd-level 3DWT representation yielded the best performance, with classification rates increasing from 92% to 98.67% compared to lower levels, while higher levels reduced classification rates.

The inclusion of preprocessing (median filtering for noise and hair removal) and GA-based feature selection significantly improved performance; the full system achieved 98.67% accuracy compared to 81.33% without preprocessing and feature selection.

The Genetic Algorithm fitness function parameters were optimized with a=0.8 and b=0.2 to balance classification accuracy and feature subset size, particularly to address the imbalanced nature of the PH2 database.

The study demonstrates that soft computing techniques can effectively enhance clinical decision-making for skin cancer diagnosis by providing an automated, accurate classification tool that reduces reliance on subjective visual interpretation.
important_remarks: The study addresses class imbalance in the PH2 database (Melanoma: 40 images vs. Benign: 160 images) by optimizing Genetic Algorithm fitness function parameters (a=0.8, b=0.2) to balance accuracy and feature subset size.
The PH2 dataset is small (200 images total), with only 40 melanoma cases, which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results for malignant class detection.

The proposed system was evaluated on the ISIC 2017 testing set (750 images) but was not trained on it; features were extracted using 3rd-level DWT, which may not capture the complex patterns learned by end-to-end deep learning models trained on larger datasets.

The paper does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the proposed method and existing methods, relying solely on point estimates of accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

The architecture uses a Deep Learning Neural Network (DLNN) with 10 hidden layers, ReLU activation, and gradient descent optimization, trained for 500 epochs with a learning rate of 0.01 and momentum of 0.9.
Preprocessing (median filtering for hair/noise removal) and GA-based feature selection were shown to be critical; removing them reduced accuracy from 98.67% to 81.33% on the PH2 database.

The 3rd-level 3DWT representation yielded the best performance (98.67% accuracy on PH2); lower levels lacked discriminating power, and higher levels introduced redundant data that reduced performance.

The study compares the proposed soft computing approach against traditional machine learning methods (KNN, SVM, NN ensemble) and recent CNN-based approaches (ResNet, Hybrid CNNs), claiming superior performance on both datasets.
No external validation on independent clinical datasets outside of PH2 and ISIC 2017 was performed, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical deployment robustness.
